data.title: American Bitcoin: A High-Flying Mining Story With A Valuation That's Running Out Of Road
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I assign American Bitcoin Corp. (NASDAQ:ABTC) a hold rating because, although the company has strengthening operational momentum, the stock price is already way ahead of its current net asset value. The fleet upgrade that has pushed the company's hashrate to 25 EH/s, along with its rising Bitcoin output and positive EBITDA, shows a company that is moving in the right direction. Nevertheless, the balance sheet remains burdened by miner purchases and lease obligations and, thus, the company's share price reflects the expectations of future expansion rather than the value of the Bitcoin the company currently holds. So, until the company can prove that it can scale toward its current 50 EH/s target while, at the same time, managing its liabilities, its risk-reward profile remains balanced. Therefore, I recommend for investors to wait for a better entry point or for further execution before taking a stronger stance toward the company.

Background & Latest News

American Bitcoin came to market on September 3, 2025 through a merger with Gryphon Digital Mining, and the pitch was a simple one: build one of the most efficient mining fleets in North America, and use those fleets to mine and accumulate a large and long-term Bitcoin (BTC-USD) reserve—a plan that the company has quickly jumped on.

Per the company's latest 10-Q, and as of November 13, 2025, American Bitcoin has accumulated approximately 4,090 Bitcoin in reserve and has the mining power of a cumulative hashrate of 25.0 EH/s, up from its previous 10.0 EH/s, across four sites—all this while upgrading their fleet which resulted in higher efficiency Antminer S21+ miners that, ultimately, improved their Bitcoin mining operations.

Despite these technological improvements, the company's stock has had other plans. While company stock opened trading above $8 back in September, it has since fallen to the $2 range that included a 37% drop in a single day. But this decline wasn't due to any operational issues; rather, the decline was due to selling pressure that was caused by early investors/shareholders selling as their lock-up period expired which, essentially, placed a heavy supply of shares into a thin float and overwhelmed demand. Bitcoin's volatility obviously added to the anxiety, but, in sum, the selling was due to too much supply hitting too little demand as opposed to anything that has to do with the company's fundamentals.

The result of all this selling pressure is a widening gap between the company's growing mining capacity and Bitcoin stack and the market's decreasing view of the company's equity.

Latest Quarterly Results & Critical Financials

American Bitcoin's latest quarter shows a company that is going in one direction: up—even though the stock has been on its way down. Per the latest 10-Q, revenue came in at $64.2 million, up dramatically from last year's $11.6 million. The drivers for this revenue growth was from the previously mentioned fleet upgrade to S21+ miners, which added a cumulative 15 EH/s, and a higher uptime across all sites. And, in total, the company mined 563 Bitcoin in Q3 versus last year's Q3 total of 190 and earned far more per coin due to a more expensive price per Bitcoin.

Cost of revenue also rose to $28.3 million due to a higher energy use and a rise in the price of power. And depreciation more than tripled due to the company buying its larger, more advanced fleet. General and administrative expenses also increased because of the company's one-time merger costs as opposed to something such as a bloated overhead. So, although operating expenses are now elevated, they are not expected to repeat.

I do want to add something that I believe is important: One area of the 10-Q that may have distorted the company's results, and maybe even confused some investors, was the company's loss on digital assets. However, this was more so an accounting issue and not a business issue. What I mean is this: When American Bitcoin became a standalone company, they had to start their Bitcoin reserve from scratch—this coming directly from the company's MD&A that I'll quote below:

On March 31, 2025, in connection with the Transactions, Parent’s strategic Bitcoin reserve remained with Parent. Therefore, immediately following the effectuation of the Transactions on March 31, 2025, the Company had no Bitcoin in reserve. Starting April 1, 2025, the Company began to build its own strategic Bitcoin reserve.

Thus, since the value of these coins are marked to market each quarter, if Bitcoin's price ends the quarter below the level where those coins were added, then the company must record a loss on paper even though the company sold no coins and no cash actually left the business. Thus, in sum, none of this affects the company's cash flow from mining.

On a headline basis, the company reported $3.5 million in net income for the quarter versus last year's Q3 loss, and, to add to that, adjusted EBITDA reached $27.7 million, that essentially flipped the company from its negative territory in 2024 and highlights the operating leverage of the company's upgraded fleet.

In sum, I believe the quarter showed four important qualities every investor should look for in these types of companies: a stronger mining output, a rise in efficiency, clearer margins, and an improved earnings power. Thus, we have a company whose financials are strengthening even though the stock price is falling, thereby widening the gap between fundamental performance and investor perception.

Growth Drivers and Catalysts

The company's path forward is shaped by two primary forces: a mining fleet that is currently scaling rapidly and a Bitcoin market that continues to tighten. Let's start with the mining fleet.

American Bitcoin's Mining Fleet

American Bitcoin now controls not only one of the largest—but also one of the most efficient—mining fleets in North America, one that I have already mentioned has jumped up from its previous 10.0 EH/s to its current cumulative hashrate of 25.0 EH/s after deploying 14.86 EH/s of new Bitmain Mining S21+ units. What these machines allow the company to do is to mine more Bitcoin per watt, thereby giving the company a cost edge while allowing them to accumulate coins in years where network difficulty may arise or the price of Bitcoin wobbles.

Thus, the company's three-fold strategy of improving mining efficiency, avoiding selling, and compounding their reserve has pushed American Bitcoin holdings above 4,000 Bitcoin. On top of that, the company also has the ability to raise cash gradually though its at-the-market [ATM] program—launched in Q3 and noted as one of the company's Q3 2025 highlights in their 10-Q—and, therefore, has the flexibility to keep expanding its fleet without taking on any expensive debt or, especially, selling off its Bitcoin reserves. And management's path to 50 EH/s, if reached, would place the company among the largest publicly listed miners.

A Tightening Bitcoin Market

Meanwhile, the other half of the growth driver story for the company is in the asset American Bitcoin produces. I wrote an article on Bitcoin last month where I mentioned the three major drivers, or forces, that are defining Bitcoin's value going into the future. Thus, I will also relay them here given that these drivers are also important for the company since Bitcoin is their primary asset.

Bitcoin's available supply continues to shrink as long-term holders, ETFs, corporates, and lending markets continue to pull coins out of circulation. More than 73% of Bitcoin is being held long-term, and in-kind redemptions are routing coins into ETF vaults as opposed to exchanges. There are also upcoming spot ETF approvals in South Korea and Japan that would remove even more supply from the market. And, for miners, these structural shifts matter because with fewer coins chasing hands and more coins held in custody, the long-term value of every Bitcoin produced continues to rise.

In Sum

Taken together, American Bitcoin benefits from these two primary forces: its own operational momentum and the tightening of Bitcoin's float. Should the company continue to grow its fleet and reserve at its current pace, then it should stand to capture more upside from Bitcoin's supply-driven cycle than its falling share price is suggesting.

Valuation

Since American Bitcoin is built around accumulating Bitcoin, the easiest way to value the company is by taking a look at where the digital asset stands today. As previously stated, per the latest 10-Q, American Bitcoin has accumulated approximately 4,090 Bitcoin in reserve that is worth around $376.8 million valued at the current Bitcoin price of $92,140. When we add back the company's cash of around $7.98 million, we get a total liquid asset base of $384.82 million; however, we still need to look at the company's obligations.

The latest Q3 balance sheet shows a company with $587.37 million in total liabilities that is driven primarily by miner-purchase obligations and lease liabilities that are tied directly to the company's fleet expansion. Thus, below is a table of the math:

Therefore, on a strict net asset basis, American Bitcoin is deeply overvalued simply because its current liabilities, at least for now, exceed the value of its Bitcoin and cash, thereby proving mathematically that the stock is not trading at its present net worth; rather, the company is trading on the expectations of future mining growth, reserve compounding, and a strong Bitcoin cycle.

Thesis Risk

The biggest risk to American Bitcoin, so far that I can see, is its balance sheet. The company's miner-purchase obligations and lease liabilities loom large relative to the value of the company's Bitcoin and, should Bitcoin's price tumble or even remain flat for a few quarters, the company could inevitably face a tighter cash position while still needing to fund further fleet expansion. And, should there be any delay in reaching its 50 EH/s target, then that could weaken the core argument that the company's future production currently justifies today's equity premium.

A second risk for the company lies in network difficulty. Bitcoin mining difficulty is continuing to rise as there is more capacity coming online globally. If the company's efficiency gains fail to keep pace with these increases in difficulty, then the number of Bitcoins mined per EH/s will fall, thereby pressuring margins and slowing down the company's reserve growth. Even small changes in difficulty can meaningfully alter the economics for any miner operating with large capital commitments.

A third risk for the company lies in stock dilution. The company's at-the-market program gives the company flexibility, but it also means that future expansion could come at the cost of issuing new shares, which would thereby reduce per-share value unless the company's mining output grows faster than its dilution.

And, finally, there are always risks that come from macro conditions. For example, a sharp drop in Bitcoin, a freeze in credit markets, or stress in crypto-linked financial products could, no doubt, reduce liquidity and push investors out of high-beta mining stocks.

Conclusion & Investor Takeaway

To summarize, American Bitcoin is executing well operationally, and it has a larger, more efficient fleet to go along with its rising Bitcoin production. But the stock's sharp disconnect from its net asset value means that investors are, as of right now, paying for growth that still has yet to be proven. The company must scale toward its 50 EH/s target, manage its liabilities, and grow its Bitcoin reserve without leaning heavily on its dilution or a perfect Bitcoin cycle.

As of now, the setup is balanced: There is strong momentum on the operations side, but we also have a valuation that leaves very little margin for error. Thus, until the company shows clearer progression on expansion and balance-sheet strength, American Bitcoin remains a hold. A better entry-point, or even stronger evidence that future mining output will justify the company's current premium, would shift that view.

data.title: Trinity Capital: Despite Risks, 14% Yield Worth Considering
data.description:
There are lots of ways to earn big yield in this market, and the BDC space (business development companies) is ripe with opportunities. And while BDCs come in a wide variety of shapes and sizes (data is shared in this report), one that stands out is Trinity Capital (TRIN) because of its growth-sector niche (arguably better aligned with the “new” US economy), internal management team (less conflicts of interest) and outstanding 14% dividend yield (covered by net investment income and backed by growing book value). This report reviews all of that, plus the big risks Trinity currently faces, and then concludes with my strong opinion on investing.

Overview:

Trinity Capital provides diversified financial solutions to growth-stage companies (i.e. mainly loans, equipment financing and “equity & warrants”), and it operates as an internally managed BDC.

For some background, BDCs were formed by an act of Congress in 1980 to help small (middle market) businesses access capital. As such, BDCs are not taxed at the corporate level as long as they pay out at least 90% of their earnings as dividends. And this is basically the reason so many income-focused investors love BDCs (i.e. the big dividend yields, as you can see in the following table).

And what makes Trinity unique in the BDC space is its focus on innovative technology (such as AI, software and space technologies—as you can see in the earlier graphic) as well as disruptive healthcare sectors of the economy. This separates Trinity from other BDCs (who are often more focused on older economic sectors) and it has also allowed Trinity to post some relatively impressive total returns (dividends—as if they were reinvested—plus price gains) in recent years as compared to other BDCs. You can see a sample of Trinity investment examples in the graphic below.

Also worth mentioning, Trinity is often granted warrants (the right to buy equity at attractive prices in the future) as part of the financing arrangements they provide to these companies; this can serve both parties well (i.e. the company, because they don’t have to immediately give away a dilutive ownership stake in their business, and Trinity because they get some attractive equity upside too—in addition to the attractive rates they are earnings on the loans they provide—as you can see in the graphic below—this is a big part of how they are able to pay investors such a big quarterly dividend yield).

Strong Financial in a Challenging Market

Despite headwinds for BDCs in general (such as economic slowdowns and easing rates—more on this in the risks section of this report), the current market environment has been strong for Trinity. For example, Trinity originated a record $773 million in Q3 commitments, thereby pushing their year-to-date total over $1.3 billion.

Further, total investment income climbed 22.3% year-over-year to $75.6 million, with NII up 29% to $37 million ($0.52/share—which exceeds the $0.51 cent quarterly dividend). Additionally, Trinity is demonstrating prudent credit quality (the BDC maintains an investment grade credit rating) in a risky landscape. For example, Trinity's weighted-average risk rating (its own internal metric) held steady at 2.9 (on a 1-5 scale) through Q3 (reflecting continued discipline in underwriting standards—amid modest sector-wide non-accrual increases). This resilience counters 2025's credit crunch, where other leveraged borrowers have been strained under persistent high rates).

And for a little more perspective, you can see the non-accrual percentages (basically the percent of loans not paying as per their loan terms—i.e. technical default) along with various other important BDCs metrics across the industry (i.e. peers and competitors of Trinity) in the following table (lots of good data here).

Dividend Safety

As mentioned, Trinity’s net investment income continues to cover its quarterly dividend payments to shareholders (a good thing), but it also has a growing history of steady dividend increases (as you can see in the chart below).

Just because a company is increasing its dividend, that is not necessarily a good thing. However, Trinity has been increasing its net asset value (see chart below) and its net investment income too, which does lend support to the attractiveness of this big-dividend BDC.

Valuation:

Currently trading at approximately 1.1x book value, Trinity is fairly reasonably priced compared to its own history (see earlier BDC table for range) and peers, especially considering the aggregate BDC book value keeps growing (while book value per share is also trending slightly higher).

And the eight Wall Street analysts covering Trinity rate it a “Buy” (with a 1.89 aggregate rating, and 10.4% share priced upside) as you can see in our earlier BDC comparison table.

Trinity Risks:

However, Trinity also faces risks investors should consider. For example, primary concerns include:

Interest Rates are a risk factor investors need to consider. In particular, Trinity has an 82.4% floating rate debt investment portfolio and a 40.6% floating rate borrowing portfolio (as of September 30, 2025). And with uncertainty surrounding the rate (and even direction) of upcoming Fed interest rate moves, Trinity is at risk, as you can see in the following graphics from the most recent investor presentation and 10Q (p 135).

Anticipated Fed cuts could pressure lending spreads and erode net investment income as BDCs refinance (likely at lower yields). You can see the percent floating versus fixed rates for most BDCs in our earlier table.

Credit defaults pose another threat. And while Trinity's non-accruals are low (as described earlier), broader private credit turmoil—from bankruptcies to tariff-impacted sectors—could hit its venture-backed holdings, especially with Trinity’s high payout ratio (i.e. limited buffer).

Lower 1st Lien Loans pose a risk. As you can see in the earlier BDC comparison table, Trinity sits at 76.6% on this metric, whereas other BDCs are considerably higher. First lien loans are considered safer (higher in the capital structure) and have lower default rates (as compared to secondary and tertiary liens and/or non-recourse loans).

Concentrated Growth Sector Exposure: With its concentration in growth and technology sectors, Trinity has been benefiting from the same “Growth/AI Bubble” that has many investors concerned. Even though Trinity is largely a debt investor, the BDC will still face pressures if/when the market cycle turns against growth and technology. For example, when the “Covid Bubble” burst in late 2021/2022, Trinity sold of hard as you can see in the following chart.

However and arguably, if the Fed plays its monetary policy cards right (with rate cuts), Trinity may not suffer the same fate (lower rates are good all sectors of the economy, especially growth and technology).

The Bottom Line

Whereas a lot of other BDCs focus on “old” economic sectors, Trinity’s niche focus on growthier and technology sectors gives its investors differentiated big-yield exposure. Not only is the outsized dividend yield attractive, but it’s generated from “newer” parts of the economy—arguably providing significantly more long-term growth potential.

However, the differentiated high income also comes with risks, including potential pain when the current growth-sector rally sells off (potentially significantly harder than more-value-focused BDCs) as we saw for Trinity when the covid-growth-bubble burst in 2021-2022 (see Trinity’s share price performance above).

Overall, I view Trinity as an attractive big-yield BDC that is absolutely worth considering. I own it (in my prudently diversified, long-term, income-focused portfolio).

data.title: Dow: Better To Bet On The Sector, Not The Company
data.description:
Introduction

It has been a couple of months since I last had a look at Dow Chemicals (NYSE: DOW) and the company posted a total return of negative 2.4% since my last analysis. It has been a tricky period for the chemical industry, and with Dow cutting its dividend to preserve cash flows and debt levels, it could be one of the best positioned to capture the upcycle.

With Dow Chemicals releasing its earnings in late October, as well as the continued soft demand for the industry, I want to see if the company is still worth a Buy rating, especially with an impressive forward yield of 5.87% that could reward patient investors.

Current Dynamics

I’ll first start with the latest earnings, with Dow’s results still reflecting the continued cyclical weakness, with sales declining across all segments. Dow actually rallied after the announcement as cash flow generation improved following the cost-cutting measures, as well as falling unit costs. Dow released a mixed set of earnings, with a miss of $230MM on a revenue of $10B, while Q3 Non-GAAP EPS came in at negative 19 cents, a beat of $0.11. Dow demonstrated some operational improvement, with around $400MM in cost savings that were clearly in line with the Q3 performance, with EBIT actually increasing by around $200MM compared to the second quarter. More so, new production assets such as the new polyethylene unit have helped contribute to sequential volume growth.

There are still external factors that have put weakness through Dow’s business, with local prices being down 8% Y/Y due to the soft global demand and actual oversupply mainly in basic chemicals. Diving deeper into segments, Packaging & Specialty plastics saw net sales decrease by 11% mainly due ot lower realized prices. Performance Materials & Coatings saw continued weak siloxane prices, mainly from oversupply in Asia. This has made management cautious about their incoming guidance with Q4 EBITDA coming in at around $725MM, and the company stated that they were committed to deliver more than $6.5B in near-term cash support, such as a $1B reducition in CapEx for the year, which I believe is the right move in this current market dynamic. In all, Q3 told us that Dow is making critical steps to cut costs and improve efficiency at the right time to capture a potential rebound in the chemical industry.

On another note, European electricity has kept creeping up above China and the US, which has been one of the key markets for Dow Chemical, and I expect this to continue, further reducing the competitiveness of power-hungry industries such as chemicals. Dow has, in a sort bypassed those issues with the closing of the company’s plants on the continent. That has helped Dow avoid running them at a loss with the current dynamics or even at lower margins than the ones on the Gulf coast that are deserving of increased expansion capital.

Valuation

The whole industry is in a doldrums, that’s for sure, with many reporting a loss over the past twelve months. Dow Inc. is no different, yet it currently has an EV/EBITDA of 9.4, around the middle of the peers pack with LYB and Westlake being marginally higher. FCF generation is currently negative for the past twelve months, but as stated earlier, thanks to the cost-cutting measures, Dow Inc. has been able to generate around $521MM in FCF for Q3, the highest since Q4 2023, and I believe that the company can continue at that pace despite the industry still not rebounding. As I mentioned, Dow closed its European facilities, and I believe that this could actually accelerate the turnaround for the industry as it fixes the oversupply issues while focusing on higher-margin facilities across Asia and the Americas. Although many sold the stock based on the dividend cut, I believe that it was the right choice to preserve the company’s financial flexibility, and it still offers a great yield at current prices.

Growth is the main question for Dow Inc. There are a lot of negatives at this particular juncture, with metrics painting a grim picture. I’m particularly bullish on the new alkoxylation units that are currently capturing demand and delivering sequential volume growth in the short-term. This unit will directly target a more resilient end market, such as personal care. On a longer-term basis, there is the Path2Zero mega-project in Saskatchewan, and though the investment is badly timed, Dow made the right decision to delay it until market conditions improve. The new ethylene cracker and the polyethylene capacity improvement should be able to deliver around $1B of EBITDA growth per year at full run rates.

Profitability at Dow is currently in dire straits as many could have picked up, with gross margin declining from 15.1% back in 2021 to 7.1% over the past twelve months. Operating margin even went into negative territory for the first time since 2019. Though Dow is currently doing its best to pick back up the pace in the recent quarter, another dynamic that I want to highlight is the fact that Dow is actually diverging from its competitors' strategy of trying to gain market share during the downturn. I’m not sure which is the best strategy, yet right now Dow’s focus on strengthening its financials seems to be the right one. Now the major headwind that I see in the coming future for Dow’s more prominent exposure to North America would be the potential rise in natural gas while oil prices fall. This would result in a lower oil-to-gas ratio that is highly indicative of the company’s potential profitability for commodity chemical producers. This would result in lower margins for the company even in a high cycle.

Risks

The highest current risk for Dow, outside of the current cycle, would be the financial risk with Dow seeing a drastic decrease in earnings power over the coming quarters. And though the company has put ample measures to secure its balance sheet which I believe will hold for the foreseeable future, the dividend might not, even as it was already decreased earlier in the year. After all, Dow’s EBITDA can not cover the current interest expense, and Net Debt/EBITDA is through the roof.

Tariffs could also become a real issue for Dow as the global trade disarray creates uncertainty and thus could bottleneck Dow’s export volumes, mainly in Polyethylene. This has come into light, as Dow is currently being investigated for potential securities fraud following its Q2 release, as the company potentially made misleading statements regarding its ability to mitigate those particular headwinds.

Conclusion

Initially, I believed that Dow would still warrant a Buy rating, considering the potential turnaround for the chemical industry. Yet right now, there are still too many headwinds that the company is facing, especially as the debt situation keeps creeping up. I believe that placing a bet on the broad chemical sector could be a better option than simply buying shares in Dow at the current juncture. I downgrade my rating to a Hold for Dow.

data.title: Netflix's $82.7 Billion Move: Brilliant Strategy Or Dangerous Gamble?
data.description:
Introduction

This morning, the stock market woke up to incredible news: Netflix, Inc. (NFLX) is acquiring Warner Bros. Discovery, Inc. (WBD) for about $82.7 billion. I broke down Netflix stock not so long ago, after the previous quarter, and even then, I gave it a Hold rating, mostly because I felt the stock was trading around fair value. Their numbers are strong, the cash flows are also strong, the NFLX balance sheet looks solid, but I didn’t see a big enough margin of safety to call it a Buy.

Of course, all of that only applied to “yesterday’s Netflix,” because this deal changes quite a lot for the company. This is an enormous transaction, and just so you understand the scale: after a big rally and while I think Netflix is overvalued, the market cap of the company is around $430 billion, and it is now buying an asset worth over $80 billion. That’s huge. As of the time of the acquisition, Netflix has around $9 billion in cash and roughly $17 billion in total debt. So the company is leveraged, but not extremely so relative to its market cap. Although if we take into account its net income and cash flows, Netflix was in a pretty strong financial position before the acquisition. But is it “so strong” that it can just casually throw tens of billions of dollars without changing the risk profile for shareholders?

For the shareholders of Netflix, quite a lot will change if the acquisition goes through. Netflix has been buying back its own shares, which investors really liked and enjoyed, but most likely, after this deal, shareholders will have to wait for more buybacks. The main focus of the company will clearly be covering this deal and managing the debt that comes with it.

What’s Good About The Deal?

Even though the beginning of this article probably doesn’t sound very positive, I do understand why Netflix would want to buy Warner Bros. Discovery. Even the intellectual property that Warner Bros. Discovery owns could make this decision worth it for Netflix one day. We are talking about Harry Potter, which I think is the biggest one, Game of Thrones with its huge untapped potential, The Big Bang Theory, The Sopranos, The Wizard of Oz, the DC universe, and a very long list of other rights that will now effectively move to Netflix.

What could Netflix do with all of that? Well, a lot. First of all, it will now be collecting money from all the merchandising tied to those franchises, which is already a very nice additional revenue source. Beyond that, Netflix can basically do whatever it wants with these brands: sequels, prequels, spinoffs, reboots, animated shows, and even selling or developing rights for gaming. Writing my previous article, I didn’t expect Netflix to go this aggressively into owning intellectual property, to be honest. I actually thought there was a strong chance the company would focus more on expanding into gaming and maybe different types of streaming. So it was definitely unexpected for me to see that Netflix fought for this deal and that it went all the way through with it, and, importantly, that it was ready to pay such a huge price.

Another positive way to look at it is that this will make Netflix a vertically integrated global entertainment leader, finally able to compete directly with Apple, Amazon, Disney, and the rest of the stronger competitors. Besides the intellectual property, Netflix gets HBO Max and all of its subscribers (more than 100 million people), it gets film and TV studios, and even some talent for filming and screenwriting.

The Deal

The deal goes for about $82.7 billion. Netflix will buy WBD for $27.75 per share, which is not cheap by any metric. Even at around $24.50 per share before the announcement, the valuation already looked quite expensive for a very old media company that hasn’t been operating particularly well.

If you open the income statement of Warner Bros. Discovery, you’ll see a business that is highly leveraged. On roughly $60 billion of market cap, WBD has more than $33 billion of debt and very little cash, and the interest expense on that debt essentially eats up most of the company’s profits. As a result, for the last three years, the business hasn’t been consistently profitable. So when you put all of that together, it really is a very interesting and, in my opinion, very risky decision on Netflix’s side.

If we take into account that Netflix does not have the cash for a deal of this size, things look even riskier. At the moment of the deal announcement, Netflix had about $9.3 billion in cash and around $17 billion in debt, which basically means it will need to raise a lot more debt. Netflix said it will pay $23.25 in cash out of the $27.75 per WBD share and $4.50 in Netflix common stock for each share of WBD. So, first of all, existing Netflix shareholders are getting dilution. Second, Netflix has already said it is taking large bridge loans from investment banks, likely somewhere around $50 billion in total, which is obviously a lot. So, the less optimistic way to look at it is that Netflix is loading up on leverage, diluting its shareholders, and betting a huge amount of money on an integration that may or may not go well.

Risks

And that brings us to what I see as one of the major risks of this deal: integration. Merging two very different companies, not only by size and what they do, but also by workers, cultures, management style, technology, and workflows, is an extremely tough job. Unless Netflix basically decides to “kill” most of Warner Bros. Discovery as an organization and only extract the intellectual property, which I don’t think they will do, this will be a very complex, multi-year integration. They are getting HBO and HBO Max, and they will most likely start creating bundled offerings, and as they put it, “cutting customer costs” (I am very doubtful that will be the case), but it will raise subscriber numbers.

I think after the deal is done, this will be a very different Netflix from the one investors were buying before. Up until now, you could argue about which direction the company might choose: gaming, live sports, more advertising, or some kind of bundling. There were a lot of options. After this announcement, at least for the next few years, the direction is very clear: Netflix will be focused on closing this acquisition, integrating Warner Bros. Discovery, and dealing with the debt and restructuring that come with it.

I don’t think Netflix will necessarily fall apart, though. I don’t think this is some existential mistake that cannot be overcome. Netflix can, in theory, execute on this very well and come out of it as a much stronger, more diversified, more powerful media company. But there are major risks involved, and personally, I don’t want to be holding the stock while all of this is happening.

Closing Thoughts

I started covering Netflix only after the previous earnings report, and I rated it a Hold back then. I definitely skipped the big rally of the last few years, and I wasn’t a shareholder, so I didn’t feel the urge to jump in at these levels. Since that article, the stock is down about 9%, and today it is down even more. As I’m writing this, Netflix has fallen below $100 per share after trading over $130 in the summer, so I feel comfortable saying that my Hold rating was justified, and I am pretty happy I didn’t chase it higher.

I wouldn’t be buying the stock here, and I wouldn’t be happy to be a shareholder during this acquisition. I think the stock has a strong chance of going down further because, for a while, we are not going to see big buybacks, we are not going to see major new investment announcements in other areas, and almost everything the company does will relate to this one deal, which is extremely expensive. It is expensive even if we take into account that Netflix currently is generating about $10.5 billion on a trailing twelve-month basis, which is a lot.

I know that some Warner Bros. Discovery shareholders were actually hoping to see an even higher bid, but $27.75 per share, in my opinion, is already a huge price for what Netflix is getting. The company has been struggling, and this will not be an easy acquisition. Acquisitions often get messy, and companies that try to merge while preserving both sides usually have many difficulties. If Netflix decides to basically strip Warner Bros. down to its intellectual property and ignore most of the rest, the integration becomes simpler, but then you’re effectively paying more than $80 billion mainly for IP, which is a staggering number, even for some of the greatest franchises ever created.

So this is a very interesting time to follow Netflix. I will definitely be watching the stock even more closely after this. We are not guaranteed that the deal will go through, because regulators are already looking at it, and there are discussions that antitrust authorities may be against such a large combination, even though, personally, I don’t fully see how that will make Netflix the largest player that could control the pricing.

If the transaction closes, we will be looking at an enormous streaming company with something like 400 million combined subscribers once you integrate 300 million Netflix users and around 100 million from HBO Max and related services. As a user, I should probably be happy because this likely means more content for me. If I were a shareholder, I wouldn’t be happy. I am glad I’m not holding Netflix right now, and I think the stock has significant downside risk from here, because the company is expensive, it is about to become heavily leveraged, and for the next few years, a lot will depend on how this one bet plays out.

data.title: DocuSign: A Value Buy As Net Retention Improves
data.description:
Markets are roaring into year end, but rather than take this as a sign of continued upside for 2026, I’m taking the opportunity to deploy careful stock picking to rotate into more rebound plays as we approach a new year. Amid the AI gold rush this year, many of the most popular software investments of the COVID era have fallen into bona fide value territory, and I think there’s a strong chance for these contrarian plays to gain upside next year.

Docusign (DOCU), in particular, is worth a second look. The e-signature company just fell over 5% after reporting a Q3 beat-and-raise despite healthy billings metrics, bringing the stock’s YTD losses to over 25%. It’s a good time for opportunistic investors with a lot of patience to reassess the bull case in this name.

I last wrote a buy article on DocuSign in September, when the stock was hovering around the low $80s. The company has given up all of last quarter’s gains; especially as hopes of a near term buyout have faded, at least for now. But as multiples continue to fall amid strong results, I’m reiterating my buy rating on this company.

The number one factor that investors should consider is that, despite a choppy rollout, DocuSign’s embrace of a more holistic platform strategy appears to be working. As a reminder, the company now brands its core platform as IAM or “intelligent agreement management.” It has various products that fall under three core buckets of creating agreements, getting customer commits (signatures), and managing customer relationships and helping companies to obtain renewals and getting reporting and analytics on customer behavior. The company now has multiple monetization streams and paths to upselling customers, not just by adding paid seats but also volume-based depending on number of agreements and contracts managed. The platform strategy is resonating, as shown by the company’s rebound to positive net retention rates and healthy renewal activity that is leading to expanded and upsized deals.

Despite a rebound in its sales momentum, DocuSign’s slide this year has left the stock in true bargain territory. At current post-Q3 share prices near $67, DocuSign trades at a market cap of $14.37 billion. After we net off the $1.05 billion of cash on Docusign’s balance sheet, the company’s resulting enterprise value is $13.32 billion.

For FY26, DocuSign has updated its full year revenue guidance to $3.208-$3.212 billion, or 8% growth versus a previous 7% expectation. It’s also expecting 9% billings growth, two points stronger than 7% previously. And the company is now expecting further efficiency, with a pro forma operating margin of 29.8-29.9% that is 80bps higher at the midpoint versus previous guidance.

For FY27 (the year ending in January 2027), Wall Street is expecting DocuSign to generate $4.04 in pro forma EPS (+10% y/y) on $3.42 billion in revenue, or 7% y/y growth. Considering the prior two quarters showed double digit billings growth, we consider this forecast to be on the conservative side.

Docusign’s valuation multiples sit at 3.9x EV/FY27 revenue and 16.6x FY26 P/E (or 15.4x on an ex cash basis). For a company that is on the cusp of being a “Rule of 40” software stock again, has a recurring revenue base with a return to net expansion trends, and significant international growth opportunity, I’d consider Docusign’s current multiples to represent a fairly de-risked entry point.

Take advantage of this dip and view it as a buying opportunity.

Q3 download

Let’s now go through DocuSign’s latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q3 earnings summary is shown below:

DocuSign’s revenue jumped 8% y/y to $814.3 million, which came in ahead of Wall Street’s expectations of $817.4 million (+7% y/y) while largely keeping pace with last quarter’s 9% growth pace.

DocuSign's billings, meanwhile, notched its second straight quarter of double-digit growth at 10% y/y. Meanwhile, trailing twelve-month billings improved to 10% y/y, which provides ample support for beating consensus' current 7% growth expectations for next year.

As seasoned software investors are aware, billings is the best forward-looking indicator for a subscription software company's growth trajectory, as it captures deals signed in the quarter that will be recognized as revenue in future quarters.

Now, DocuSign did note that a number of earlier-than-expected renewals boosted DocuSign's billings growth rate in Q3. That said, the company also faced a tough comp from a pull-in of renewals in Q3 of last year.

Per CFO Blake Grayson's remarks on the Q3 earnings call:

Billings outperformance was primarily driven by 2 elements. The first was renewal timing and early renewal strength, which drove slightly more than half of the outperformance in Q3. Similar to Q2, we saw slightly higher early renewals than forecasted. Importantly, the quality of those early renewals continued to improve year-over-year as a percentage of early renewals with expansion grew and the share of early renewals that were flat, declined.

The second element was a collection of smaller impacts, including a small shift in payment frequency to annual bookings performance and slight FX favorability.

When removing the impact from timing relative to our forecast, billings growth for Q3 was approximately 8% year-over-year. As a reminder, we also saw elevated early renewal activity in the second half of fiscal 2025, creating a more difficult year-over-year billings comparison in Q3 and Q4 of this year."

That said, we still view Q3 as a strong quarter, especially with management's commentary that renewals tended to lead to expansion deals, which helped the company's net revenue retention rates improve by 2 points y/y to 102%.

We note that DocuSign is planning on discontinuing billings reporting in FY27, due to the lumpiness that renewal timing has caused. While the company will no longer guide to this metric, we do note that we will still be able to pull together calculated billings from the company's financial statements (revenue plus the change in deferred revenue), so no visibility has been lost.

On the profitability front, DocuSign cut sales and marketing costs by 160bps as a percentage of revenue, and general and administrative costs by a further 90bps, helping to catalyze a 180bps y/y rise in pro forma operating margins to 31.4%.

Key takeaways

We note that when stacked on top of 8.4% revenue growth, DocuSign sits right at the cusp of the Rule of 40, reflecting a company that is at a more measured, mature pace of growth but that has also turned its focus to driving balanced operating leverage. DocuSign's ~15x ex-cash P/E and ~4x revenue multiple substantially undervalue a business that is regaining sales momentum. Stay long here.

data.title: REIT Bulls - Brace For More Frustration
data.description:
Over the past 3 years, REITs (VNQ) have delivered only 16% in total returns, while the overall stock market (SPY) has expanded by roughly 75%. If we adjust for the inflation, the real return figure from REITs is much lower.

There have been multiple drivers behind REIT underperformance. Apart from the fact that the benchmark (i.e., SPY) here has itself registered abnormal returns, the biggest driver for why even on an absolute and inflation-adjusted level REITs have delivered unsatisfactory results is the elevated interest rate curve.

For example, the fact that the fed funds rate has gone from being close to zero in early 2022 to ~5.33% in Q3, 2023 did not help REITs, which are inherently interest rate-sensitive vehicles. Similarly, the long-term (U.S. 10-year Treasury) yield level jumping from ~1.8% to ~4% has not helped either.

In the meantime, during the past 3-year period, REIT earnings have grown. Not by much, but still they are higher than where they were before.

Here is a visual example of how the FFO dynamics look in practice for Agree Realty Corporation (ADC), EPR Properties (EPR), Essential Properties Realty Trust (EPRT), and STAG Industrial, Inc. (STAG):

Granted, there are some exceptions, such as Alexandria Real Estate Equities, Inc. (ARE), Armada Hoffler Properties, Inc. (AHH), and multiple hotel and lodging REITs, which have experienced significant FFO compression. But this is only logical and how it should be in any sector, in any asset class.

So if we put these two things together, we can arrive at a perfect setup for making a huge bet on REITs (and I have not yet even added the key driver here).

Namely, the interest rates have gone up, which has triggered valuation compression, while the earnings, on the ground, have continued to increase and, importantly, are projected to rise going forward.

Now what could really catapult REIT prices and valuations closer to the historical averages is the negatively sloped future interest rate curve.

The logic is simple: given that the higher rates were the main driver for subpar REIT performance, lower rates should stimulate the opposite process.

And this is where I disagree with the REIT bulls.

What the REIT Bulls Are Missing

Let's understand something important about how the REIT valuations work.

REITs, at their core, are hybrid instruments, sharing some properties with common equities and some with fixed income (bonds). The fact that they can issue equity, take on more debt, and reinvest cash flows to grow renders REITs similar to equities. Yet the requirement to distribute most of the profits in dividends, the nature of most leases (long-term contracts with relatively fixed payments), and the inherently slow growth asset base (real estate) push REITs closer to fixed-income instruments, which embody a high duration factor.

And for fixed-income instruments, due to their high duration profiles (i.e., price/value sensitivity to interest rate moves), the question of what happens with the interest rates is very critical.

However, there is a huge difference between the interest rate dynamics that are associated with the front end of the curve and the back end of the curve.

As most REITs - think of such household names as Realty Income Corporation (O), VICI Properties (VICI), ADC, EPRT, and NNN REIT, Inc. (NNN) - have ventured into long-term lease contracts with their tenants, their duration profiles have naturally gravitated closer to the long-term interest rate segment.

To put it differently, the changes in the base rates, which are the ones that the Fed can directly influence by cutting or raising, do not play that important a role as what happens at the back end of the curve.

So the question is whether the long-term yields will also come down in a correlated fashion with the very likely decreasing base rates.

My guess is that it will not happen, or at least not in such magnitude as the base rates could potentially be cut by the Fed. In fact, I would not even rule out a scenario in which we see inflating long-term yields as the Fed ventures into incremental cuts.

Let me quickly explain this.

Since 2023, we can see a constant rise in the spread between the long-term yields and the short-term yields:

The main or structural driver is the concerns about the U.S. fiscal policy, where the deficits remain stubbornly deep, thereby adding more and more debt to the government's books. The idea was that the tariff policy would help combat this, but the emerging ideas, like handing out $2,000 checks to U.S. households, go clearly against that.

Another aspect that we have to keep in mind is what happens with the market's confidence in the U.S. if there are suddenly very aggressive base rate cuts by the newly elected Fed chair (it is not a low probability case). It might further accelerate dollar depreciation, causing international demand for the U.S. Treasury to plummet even further. For example, the last time when the Fed cut the interest rates, the long-term yields actually jumped slightly higher.

What we have to appreciate on top of these Fed-driven dynamics are the corporate credit spreads, which are currently at their historical lows:

If the corporate bond spreads start to mean-revert, even by a bit, then that will be just another headwind for REIT prices. What could trigger spread expansion is a weakening economy, a struggling labor market, and/or rising corporate defaults.

Finally, let me close the topic with the following chart:

In this chart we can actually see that, from a historical perspective, REIT yields relative to the U.S. 10-year yields are unattractive (too low). To get back to the average yield spread, REITs would have to start producing higher yields (which would imply further declines in valuation multiples), or the U.S. Treasury yield would have to decline.

Two things:

  • Given the facts that I outlined in the article above, I would not be that optimistic about the long-term yields going down.
  • This chart puts REIT valuations into a very different light compared to what kind of situation I plotted in the intro section of this article (i.e., compared to the U.S. 10-year Treasury yield, REITs are expensive).

The Bottom Line

Let's take a step back.

I would say that it does not matter so much whether REIT valuation multiples bounce back to historical levels, remain at the current levels, or enter an even lower zone.

At the end of the day, REITs are mostly income-oriented (and income growth) plays that are underpinned by real assets, which should serve as a hedge for inflation or major financial disruptions.

Of course, price appreciation is always welcomed (although, one might debate whether it does not add headwinds for reinvestment yields and income compounding), but again, it should not be the main driver for our investments in REITs.

Having said that, the bigger problem is that REIT yields are relatively unattractive if we consider inflation levels, which are closer to 3%. For example, VNQ's dividend yield is 3.9%, where the underlying dividend over the past 5-year period has been increased by ~3.6% per year.

In other words, most REITs are badly positioned in terms of absolute real income levels and the growth prospects, which do not really exceed the CPI level that much. So, assuming this and relatively flat price performance, I simply do not see a huge merit in deploying loads of capital into REITs.

Personally, I still hold several REITs in my portfolio, but these are a) included for diversification purposes and b) carefully cherry-picked names with meaningfully higher yields and balanced financial risk profiles.

data.title: UP Fintech: Turning Bullish On Q3 Growth Acceleration And Positive Full-Year Prospects (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
I've revised UP Fintech's (TIGR) rating from 'Hold' to 'Buy'.

TIGR achieved a faster pace of growth and margin expansion in Q3. Its full-year results should be excellent as well, taking into account a number of positive factors.

The mixed findings from its 2Q25 disclosures were detailed in my previous August 28, 2025 write-up.

Latest Quarter Saw Topline Acceleration And Profitability Enhancement

TIGR's 3Q25 financials were released in a 6-K published on Thursday, Dec 4.

Its Jul-Sep '25 turnover was +73% higher at $0.18B on YoY terms. That was superior as compared to the group's 2Q2025 and 3Q2024 growth rates of +59% and +44%, respectively.

In my opinion, a vibrant trading environment was the main driver of TIGR's outperformance.

There's naturally increased speculation in micro-caps and "penny shares" when the market heats up. TIGR's execution fees are tied to the quantity of shares transacted. This explains why its 3Q2025 "take rate" for stocks rose 0.7bps (basis points) year-on-year to 0.071ppts (percentage points).

Investors are more likely to purchase shares with borrowed monies, as their risk appetites get bigger. Consequently, the company's interest income expanded by +53% YoY and +25% quarter-on-quarter to $73M for the 3rd quarter of 2025.

Corporates are also rushing for IPOs to capitalize on improved market sentiment. That led to a +189% surge in TIGR's "other revenues" to 3Q25's $26M. It was the underwriter for ten U.S./HK public listings during this period.

Separately, the firm's normalized net margin widened by +13ppts YoY and +1ppts sequentially to 33% in the recent three-month period. This also meant that its net profit went from 3Q24's $20M to 3Q2025's $57M.

The positive effects of fixed-cost leverage and an optimal change in user mix boosted profitability.

TIGR's Q3 media release emphasized that it has been "earning stronger trust from high-quality clients." It also revealed that "client assets in these two markets (Singapore and Hong Kong) grew approximately 20% and 60% QoQ (in 3Q25 respectively), highlighting a steady improvement in overall user quality."

Also, there are negligible incremental expenses incurred to serve additional customers with its tech-focused self-service trading platform. This translates into scale economies and higher margins for the group.

Its shares had gone up 4% on the day of results announcement. The investing community was clearly impressed with TIGR's Q3 showing. I take the same view too.

Anticipating Solid FY25 Numbers

TIGR is expected to report its full-year results in March next year.

The sell-side is predicting a +93% jump in the company's non-GAAP EPS to FY2025's $0.82 as per S&P Capital IQ. Its bottom-line growth in the preceding year was a relatively slower +57%.

I'm optimistic about TIGR's prospects.

A thing to watch is the firm's good performance in Q4 thus far.

At the analyst briefing, TIGR shared that "our trading volume for the first 2 months of the fourth quarter is already on par with the entire Q3." Its "net asset inflow quarter-to-date remains robust, and are expected to be slightly better than the Q3" according to management's call remarks.

The above-mentioned trends suggest that its positive momentum from 3Q will most probably be sustainable.

Also, TIGR's "Hong Kong/HK" business has reached an inflection point. The recent launch of fresh offerings in that market such as derivatives for Japan indices was well-received.

My early-Jun article noted that "the group is making headway in Hong Kong", which should lead to "financial improvement." The write-up also referred to its Singapore unit as the "flagship."

TIGR disclosed in its 6-K filing that "Singapore and Hong Kong" were "each accounting for roughly 40% of (3Q2025) new funded clients." The same metric for HK was 30% in 2Q25.

It's encouraging to see the HK division closing the gap with the Singapore counterpart. The former was only started three years ago, unlike the latter's operations which were established since 2020.

Also, TIGR's earnings are benefiting from favorable regulatory development.

Earlier in the year, SEC confirmed that "starting on May 14, 2025, the fee rates applicable to most securities transactions will be set at $0.00 per million dollars."

TIGR outlined the new rule's impact at its Q3 call. It indicated that "clearing costs decreased by 17% this quarter, bringing the quarterly clearing costs to a historic low of 6%." The company also guided that "the current clearing cost rate (at six percent) is quite sustainable as we are self-clearing for all core products."

My view is that it should do as well as, if not better than what the consensus is forecasting.

You Should Consider These Risks

TIGR's future financial metrics might fall short of expectations in the event that investors go into "risk-off" mode. This can happen because of geopolitical crises or economic downturns in the U.S. or China.

Its progress in HK could be derailed, if competing brokers put up a tougher fight. The rivals may introduce new products or roll out more promotions to stem the loss of market share to TIGR.

My Conclusion

I'm a bull on the name now.

According to S&P Capital IQ data, TIGR trades at 10.6 times FY26 P/E. The same valuation ratio for Futu Holdings (FUTU) 15.1x.

The stock's potential upside is +42% (15.1/10.6), using its listed comparable as a benchmark.

I believe a similarly outstanding full-year FY25 performance can justify a more demanding multiple for TIGR.

data.title: Monster Beverage: Buy This Global Energy Drink Champion With More Room To Run
data.description:
Thesis

Monster Beverage (NASDAQ:MNST) appears to be in a new cycle of profitability and global expansion that, in my opinion, is not yet fully recognized by the market.

Management presents a combination of aggressive commercial strategy, technological upgrade in the supply chain operation and a deep understanding of the needs of the energy drinks category.

Q32025 was impressive, with sales growth and more than doubling of earnings.

The Company is strengthening its brand without sacrificing margins while increasing its presence in critical markets outside the US.

Monster has become an uncommon story, combining global scale, low production costs, product innovation and a loyal customer base.

Therefore, according to my perspective the stock justifies a premium valuation and maintains substantial upside.

Financials

Monster announced Q32025 sales growth of nearly 17%, while net income was up 41%.

International sales growth was impressive +23%, bringing the international contribution to 43% of total revenue, the highest in the company’s history.

The 250 basis point expansion in gross margins was driven by a better product mix, higher prices and more efficient manufacturing.

In my opinion, this trend is sustainable, especially as the portfolio shifts toward Zero Sugar and Ultra products, which have higher margins.

The U.S. market continued to strengthen with double-digit growth, with the Ultra brand posting a 29%+ increase.

Operating income increased nearly 41%, suggesting that the company is keeping its costs under control.

With specific price increases starting November 1, i expect Q12026 to show further margin improvement.

As i see it, Monster has two more years of strong demand and high operating leverage ahead of it.

Corporate Developments & Strategic Outlook

The company’s profile now goes beyond the narrow definition of “energy drink company.”

It is essentially transforming into a multi-level player with a global reach.

In my personal assessment, the most significant developments that are changing Monster’s strategic trajectory are three.

First, the Company is strengthening its foundation in innovation.

2026 has already been announced as one of the richest years of new products in its history, with new proposals Ultra, Juice Monster, Monster Electric Blue, while also introducing a new line exclusively for women, under the FLRT brand.

It seems to me that this move has strong strategic value, as women remain the most underserved segment of the category and could significantly accelerate overall penetration.

Second, Monster is expanding its presence in EMEA, APAC and Latin America. The EMEA region ran at a pace of over 30%, with new markets recording leading positions for the first time.

Monster became the No. 1 brand in Greece and strengthened its position in the United Kingdom, Poland, Spain, Australia and the Middle East.

In China, the increase exceeded 40%, while in India it reached 54%.

I feel that these numbers show that Monster has not been saturated, but is in an early phase in the most populous markets in the world.

Third, the technological upgrade of the supply chain is a quiet but extremely important advantage.

The company is going full digital through SAP IBP, strengthening forecasting, production and logistics.

At the same time, it is creating new flavor houses in Brazil and China, reducing costs and distribution times.

My view is that this change will enhance efficiency in the coming years and will allow the company to launch more products with less risk of production bottlenecks.

Monster is also strengthening its presence in the alcoholic RTD category through Beast, Blind Lemon and the new Stunt Double.

This market can develop into a second pillar of growth within the next five years.

Valuation

The stock is currently trading at around $74, with a P/E of around 40x.For a company that has been growing at a steady double-digit rate and increasing margins this premium is not extreme.

The stock is trading near $74 (with a P/E of 40x) and for a Company that is growing at a steady double digit and is increasing its margins this premium looks reasonable.

In the conservative scenario, Monster returns to single-digit growth and to a P/E of 30x. At that level, the stock would decrease to $55–$58. I only see this scenario in case of a serious slowdown.

In the base case scenario, the Company keeps double digit earnings growth and stable margins, with P/E near 40x.

Under this scenario, the stock would move to $80 to $85 during 2026 as earnings continue to strengthen. I consider this the most realistic outlook.In the bullish scenario, international markets gain speed, the Ultra series expands its share and Monster achieves stronger operating leverage.

P/E approaches 45x or even 50x and the stock could trade between $95-$100.In my estimation this outcome is feasible as emerging markets are driving most of the company’s momentum.

The stock is +36% this year, which may look “moderate” at first but it has developed through a solid uptrend and brief, well controlled pullbacks.

Risks

Despite Monster's strong position there are risks that need to be considered.First, the energy drinks category is highly competitive.

Companies like Red Bull or newer players can put pressure to the company with aggressive offers and heavy advertising.

As far as i am concerned, Monster maintains a lead due to the breadth of its portfolio but the pressure in the US remains.

In addition, Monster is heavily dependent on the agreements with the Coca-Cola network for distribution.

Any malfunction or restructuring of the agreements could affect its own profitability. I think that the partnership is solid but still remains a structural risk.

The geographical factor is also a challenge. Emerging markets offer great prospects but also carry higher volatility in terms of currency, regulatory changes and taxation. The recent increase in the excise tax in the Mexican market is a typical example.

Similarly, markets such as Argentina show fluctuations that affect pricing and revenue.

Finally, Monster is moving more aggressively into categories like RTDs and wellness drinks and these markets have thinner margins and intense local competition and shifting resources toward them may create added execution risk.

Conclusion

Monster is one of the most well constructed companies in the FMCG industry and still has substantial growth potential ahead of it.

Management's strategy is clear, aggressive and deeply focused on global expansion.

The energy drinks category continues to grow, while Monster shows that it can gain share in almost all geographic markets.

High margins, good pricing strategy and a strong international presence supports my view that the stock has room for growth in the base and bullish scenarios.

Monster is a clear "BUY" for me.

data.title: Meta's Next Idea Could Be Bigger Than The Smartphone
data.description:
Meta Platforms, Inc. (META) stock has declined roughly 11% since its October highs, which to me, looks less like a warning and more like a buying opportunity. Sure, investors were spooked by swelling AI-related CapEx, but Meta is funding this buildout from a position of strength, with a cash-rich balance sheet and an ad machine that is already monetizing AI at scale. Advantage+ is quietly becoming a growth engine with both pricing and impressions rising together, a combo that can compound revenue faster than the headline numbers suggest.

The bigger story, though, is what comes next. Meta is not just upgrading ad targeting, it’s laying the groundwork for a platform shift. If products like its Ray-Ban Meta glasses keep scaling, they could move Meta from being “a collection of apps on your phone” to a “computer on your face” company. If Meta gets it right, it could unlock an entirely new vertical and hardware-driven ecosystem.

In this article, I break down why the market may be missing the point of Meta’s AI spend, what the key catalysts are, and why this dip could be the setup for the next leg higher.

Spoiler: My Strong Buy rating on Meta stock is unchanged.

Stock summary

Meta stock trades at around $661 per share, down about 11% since the end of Oct. This year, the stock has gained about 13% and roughly 8% over the past 12 months. Over a longer time period, Meta has grown by around 137% over the past 5 years.

Valuation-wise, Meta trades at a FWD P/E ratio of 27.5x on a GAAP basis. For starters, this metric tells us how much investors are willing to pay for every dollar of Meta’s annualized earnings. In this case, investors are paying $27.50 per $1 of earnings. This puts Meta at a 63% premium compared to the sector median.

That said, I believe this premium is warranted given Meta’s business model. I mean, it’s one of the most reliable, mega-cap tech companies of this decade. However, Meta stock has experienced a setback recently.

Why did Meta’s share prices decline?

Meta stock started to decline in late Oct. following the company’s third-quarter earnings report. And by the looks of it, the absolute biggest driver of this drop is the significant increase in AI spending plans. Investors were spooked.

In its third-quarter earnings report, Meta raised 2025 CapEx guidance to between $70 and $72 billion- up from a previous range of $66 - $72 billion. These are the funds primarily allocated for AI-related expenses like data centers and other essential equipment. On top of that, the company announced that CapEx in 2026 would be notably larger and that total expenses would grow significantly faster than in 2025. That also spooked investors, especially those who worried about margins being squeezed.

This relatively bearish sentiment was reinforced by uncertainties that the AI sector’s spending would actually pay dividends. Market pundit, Michael Burry even raised doubts about the actual AI accounting, citing concerns that hyperscales might’ve boosted their earnings by stretching out the assumed lifespan of their infrastructure assets.

On that note, other reports suggest that Peter Thiel’s firm and Softbank (OTCPK:SFTBY) sold their Nvidia positions, which created a secular headwind for tech companies pouring heavily into AI developments, and Meta Platforms is sure one of them.

But what’s perplexing is that Meta’s third-quarter results were actually really strong. Revenue rose 26% to $51.24 billion compared to the same period last year, which also outpaced market estimates by 3.71%. Although net income on a GAAP basis declined about 83% to $2.71 billion, $1.08 a share (basic), this was driven by a one-time non-cash income tax charge of $15.93 billion due to the newly enacted One Big Beautiful Bill Act. This one-time charge also works in Meta’s favor, as it will significantly reduce U.S. federal tax payments moving forward.

According to CFO Susan Li, without this one-time hit, net income and EPS would have landed at $18.6 billion $7.25 per share, respectively.

Moreover, GAAP gross margin is 82.03%, which is quite impressive, while operating income rose 18.35% to $20.53 billion. So overall, Meta’s financial performance is solid as usual.

Going back to the CapEx factor, which I believe is the biggest driver of this bearish sentiment. I think the market is missing the bigger picture. Meta is making these investments from a position of strength, not weakness. As you might be aware, the company has a substantial free cash flow of $10.62 billion, and cash and cash equivalents of $44.45 billion against a minimal long-term debt of just around $28.8 million.

Unlike many tech and AI companies, Meta is capable of investing for growth and staying financially healthy simultaneously, which is why I’m not overly concerned about CapEx.

What could drive Meta even higher?

Buying the dip right now would be justifiable, given the potential growth drivers down the line. Starting with the most immediate catalyst:

The ad business has improved with AI

Looking at the third quarter report, Meta’s AI investments are translating into revenue growth. Again, revenue rose 26% YOY, which is also Meta’s highest revenue growth rate since the first quarter of 2024, all thanks to its AI systems creating monetization opportunities from its user base.

Specifically, Meta’s ARR for end-to-end AI-powered ad solutions surpassed $60 billion. Putting it in perspective, this is what the company would make over a full year if current performance continues, and that $60 billion annually comes from AI ad tools alone- specifically the Advantage+ suite, excluding the rest of the business.

The Advantage+ suite is a set of tools that automates advertising campaigns for businesses, and over 4 million advertisers are now using it. When you look deeper, this AI integration has driven roughly 5% more ad conversions on Instagram and 3% on Facebook, which also serves as proof that Meta’s AI is making ads more effective. As a result, advertisers are willing to spend more as they get better results. Can you blame them?

Now, if we go down a level deeper, these improvements are Meta’s GEM, or Generative Ads Recommendation Model, and Andromeda, which are both advanced AI systems that Meta developed. Andromeda, in particular, is quite interesting. It’s essentially Meta’s architecture for ad targeting and was developed in partnership with Nvidia (NVDA).

Industry reports suggest that Andromeda is 10,000x more powerful than Meta’s old systems, though that might be a little overstated. In practice, every time we scroll through our newsfeeds on Facebook or Instagram, there are tens of millions of possible ads that Meta could show us. Andromeda’s role is to narrow that down to a few, most suitable options in milliseconds, and find what’s best for our interests based on our activities online.

About a year ago, Meta reported about Andromeda’s 6% recall improvement, which measures how accurately the system finds relevant ads, and also delivered an 8% improvement in ad quality on certain segments. The company also reported advertisers experienced a 22% increase in return on ad spend (ROAS). I suppose these figures are much better-looking today.

Going back to the present, Instagram reels have also become more centralized on the app with a substantial ARR. According to CEO Mark Zuckerberg:

Video is a particular bright spot with video time spent on Instagram up more than 30% since last year. And as video continues to grow across our apps, Reels now has an annual run rate of over $50 billion. Improvements in our recommendation systems will also become even more leveraged as the volume of AI-created content grows.

Instagram Reels is Meta’s answer to TikTok, and the fact that it’s generating $50 billion annually proves that the company can adapt as user behavior shifts.

As I went deeper into the earnings call, I spotted that Meta’s ad business benefited from two things happening at once. Specifically, average ad prices increased 10% compared to the same period last year, while impressions rose 14% over the same period. When both of these go up together, it creates a compounding effect where revenue grows faster than either metric alone.

Bottom line: Meta has industrialized AI at scale while most competitors are still figuring it out.

Meta’s Ray-Ban glasses might be the next computing platform

As you might’ve read somewhere else, Meta’s smart glasses are seeing significant growth. Specifically, sales were up more than 200% in the first half of 2025, and that signals an entirely new hardware revenue stream. The company that makes these glasses is EssilorLuxottica, and has shipped over 3.5 million pairs of Meta’s smart glasses through mid-2025.

Personally, I see this as more than Meta selling new gadgets, such as what Apple (AAPL) does. If this project goes well, Meta might own the next computing platform. Some might go as far as to argue that Apple and Alphabet (GOOG/GOOGL) launched something similar in the past, like the Apple Vision Pro and Google Glass, only top flops.

Well, aesthetics-wise, these glasses make you look like a cyborg or a diver with snorkeling gear, while Meta’s smart glasses are as minimal as they can be. This is likely why it gained more traction than the early birds of the segment. Now, remember how we went from computers to smartphones? These smart glasses have the potential to be the next major shift in how we interact with tech every day, though it might still take a while until we’re all wearing them.

Not convinced? IDC forecasts sales growth of global wearables to increase from 9.4 million units in 2025 to nearly 20 million in 2029. And according to IDC’s available data, Meta already holds over 70% of the global AR and VR market, so if it continues to execute, it can capture a considerable portion of that projected sales.

And consider that with a lower price point (compared with a cell phone) and >8 billion people on the planet, it's not so difficult to imagine a world where these types of glasses become the default way to stay productive and communicate with the world.

My take

So, what’s my take?

The market has given us a buying opportunity amid risks in overspending. Would I have liked to buy at the $590 levels that we saw a couple of weeks ago? Sure. But I did need some time to digest the news. What I found was that it was the company-specific and the sector-wide concerns about AI spending likely hammered the stock- to some degree. And make no mistake, Meta’s business foundation remains solid. In fact, the numbers mentioned above support it.

That said, Meta is building multiple durable revenue streams that should compound for years. But the real upside, though, is optionality: if Ray-Ban Meta glasses continue to scale, Meta could be in the midst of creating their very own “iPhone moment,” not through a phone, but through a new interface that we wear. In my view, this dip is less a warning sign and more a setup.

This, and for all the reasons stated above, my Strong Buy rating for META remains unchanged.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Low-Fire, Low-Hire Job Market, After Massive Overhiring By Big Tech And Others In 2021 And 2022
data.description:
But the initial unemployment insurance claims were a doozie that'll reverse next week.

Initial applications for unemployment benefits (“initial claims”) in the week through Saturday plunged from already low levels, in an outlier move, by 27,000 from the prior week to 191,000, seasonally adjusted, the lowest since 2022 (blue line in the chart).

These are applications for unemployment insurance that freshly laid-off people filed at state unemployment agencies, which then reported them to the US Department of Labor by the weekly deadline, which then combined the data and published it on Thursday.

Obviously, one or more big states didn’t get their claims data filed before deadline, this having been the week of Thanksgiving, which is what caused this plunge in claims. But that data gets picked up in the next week, and so we’ll see a spike in claims, which will undo part or all of Thursday’s plunge.

The four-week average fell to 214,750, seasonally adjusted, which is historically low and in the same low range that it has been in for the past four years.

The four-week average irons out the week-to-week squiggles of weeks when deadlines were missed and weeks when the missed data was included (red line in the chart).

In a historical context going back to the 1970s, initial claims are very low, despite the growth of nonfarm payrolls over the decades. They were lower only during the tight labor market of 2018 and 2019 and during the labor shortages coming out of the pandemic.

This shows that layoffs are really not a problem in this unusual labor market.

The labor market is showing the effects of overhiring during the pandemic by companies that have since then laid off people and hired people at the same time, which is what Amazon (AMZN) and Alphabet (GOOG, GOOGL) have done, with the net effect that their headcount, after exploding for a few years, has been roughly flat - more in a moment.

And the labor market may be showing the effects of the crackdown on illegal immigration and some tightening up of legal immigration, which reduce the supply of labor and slow hiring.

The Challenger Job Cuts Report, also out this morning by outplacement firm Challenger, Gray & Christmas, tracks layoff announcements by US companies of their global workforce. So these are not actual layoffs, but announcements of potential future layoffs globally.

In November, layoff announcements fell to 71,321. The layoff announcements this year were concentrated in February (172,017), March (275,240), and October (153,074).

When Amazon announced in late October that it would lay off 30,000 people of its global workforce - with an undisclosed portion to take place in the US, and the rest globally - it pumped up the Challenger Jobs Cut report to 153,000, even though those layoffs hadn’t happened yet, and not all might happen, and even though only some of the layoffs will take place in the US, and even though Amazon was hiring through the other door.

October’s 153,000 was the highest since March, and that would not have made effective "the job market is cracking" headlines. But it was the highest October since October 2020, and that did trigger a round of the "job market is cracking" headlines.

Continued unemployment insurance claims, also released on Thursday by the Labor Department, track people who applied for unemployment insurance at least a week earlier and are still claiming unemployment insurance because they still haven’t found a job.

These “continued unemployment claims” fell in the latest week to 1.939 million, the second week in a row of declines, and are down by 29,000 from the recent high at the end of July.

The four-week average dipped for the second week in a row to 1.945 million.

Over the past four decades, it’s only during the tight labor market in 2018 and 2019 and in the years since Covid, particularly the period of the labor shortages in 2021 and 2022, that the level was lower - despite the much larger nonfarm payrolls.

It does mean that people remain on the unemployment insurance rolls a little longer than in 2022-2024 and in 2018-2019, but not nearly as long as they did in the decades before 2018.

So layoffs are very low, but once laid off, it takes people longer to find a job, as companies have slowed their hiring.

Alphabet and Amazon, along with other tech giants, exemplify this low-fire, low-hire labor market. After the massive overhiring during the pandemic, which was in part responsible for the labor shortages during that time, they started trimming some of the excess in some places, starting in 2022, while still adding in other places - and their global headcount has remained roughly stable since 2022, after exploding in prior years.

Alphabet, which discloses the headcount in its quarterly filings, reported 190,167 employees globally at the end of Q3, up from year-end 2024 and flat with year-end 2022.

Between 2016 and 2023, its headcount had tripled! During the overhiring binge in 2020-2023, its headcount had soared by 62%. These are huge increases for big, mature companies.

Amazon, which discloses global year-end headcount in its annual reports, had 1.56 million employees at year-end 2024, slightly higher than in the prior two years but below the peak in 2021 (1.61 million).

Though a tech giant, a big portion of Amazon’s employees are in brick-and-mortar retail (including Whole Foods), ecommerce retail, warehousing, and transportation around the world.

Overhiring: In the two years of 2020 and 2021, its headcount had doubled. From year-end 2015 through year-end 2021, its headcount had multiplied by seven! These are gigantic increases.

Since 2021, it has laid off people and hired people simultaneously - though it’s the layoff announcements that get into the news, a time-honored corporate strategy, eagerly spread by the crisis media, to cow restless employees.

The low-fire, low-hire labor market also shows up in employment data, where job creation has flattened out: Private-Sector Job Trends by Industry: From Job Destruction in “Information” to Job Creation in “Mining & Natural Resources”

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.
